name: Hypokalaemia
identifyBy: Flat or inverted T waves (early sign)
Prominent U waves (amplitude > T wave in same lead)
Apparent QT prolongation (actually QU interval - T and U waves merge)
PR interval may be prolonged in severe cases
management: Check serum potassium - ECG changes correlate roughly with K+ level
K+ 3.0–3.5 mmol/L (mild): oral potassium replacement if tolerated
K+ 2.5–3.0 mmol/L (moderate): IV potassium chloride 10–20 mmol/hr (max 40 mmol/hr via central line)
K+ <2.5 mmol/L or symptomatic: IV replacement, cardiac monitoring, correct co-existing hypomagnesaemia
Monitor ECG during replacement - risk of VT/VF at severe levels or with rapid correction
Identify cause: diuretics, vomiting, diarrhoea, hyperaldosteronism, renal losses
pearls: Hypokalaemia potentiates digoxin toxicity - dangerous combination even at 'therapeutic' digoxin levels
Hypomagnesaemia frequently co-exists - must replace Mg2+ or K+ replacement will fail
Prominent U wave >1mm in V2–V3, or U wave taller than T wave, is clinically significant
ECG changes lag behind serum levels - patient may have dangerous hypokalaemia with subtle ECG
Torsades de Pointes is a feared complication - especially with concurrent QT-prolonging drugs
differentials: Normal ECG variant (small U waves in V2–V3 are normal)

qaNotes: Electrolyte pattern emphasises QT/ST duration and T-wave morphology.
